learning_objectives: Translate English statements into propositional logic formulas
Construct truth tables and determine logical equivalences
Apply De Morgan's laws and other logical identities
readings: Epp, Discrete Mathematics with Applications, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Discrete mathematics is the mathematical foundation of computer science. Logic, proof, counting, graphs, and recursion underpin algorithms, databases, programming languages, and cryptography. This course gives you the mathematical tools to think precisely about computation.
key_concepts: Propositions: statements that are true or false
Logical connectives: NOT, AND, OR, implies, if and only if
Truth tables: evaluating compound propositions
Logical equivalences: tautology, contradiction, contingency
De Morgan's laws: NOT(A AND B) = NOT A OR NOT B
lecture_notes.demonstration: Translate a software requirements specification into propositional logic, then use logical equivalences to simplify and identify contradictory requirements
setup_requirements: Logic proof assistant or truth table generator

review.value: P implies Q is false only when P is true and Q is false; if P is false, the implication is vacuously true. This matches the programming contract: if precondition is met, postcondition is guaranteed; if precondition is not met, anything can happen
De Morgan's laws directly apply to boolean expressions in code: not(a and b) == (not a) or (not b). Simplifying complex conditionals using logical equivalences improves code readability

class_schedule.topic: Proof Techniques: Direct, Contrapositive, and Contradiction
learning_objectives: Construct direct proofs for conditional statements
Apply proof by contrapositive and proof by contradiction
Recognize which proof technique is appropriate for a given statement

lecture_notes.introduction: Proofs are the currency of mathematics and theoretical computer science. The ability to construct a rigorous proof means you can verify that an algorithm is correct, that a system is secure, or that a data structure maintains its invariants.
key_concepts: Direct proof: assume P, derive Q through logical steps
Proof by contrapositive: prove NOT Q implies NOT P
Proof by contradiction: assume the negation, derive a contradiction
Proof by cases: split into exhaustive cases, prove each
Common mistakes: affirming the consequent, circular reasoning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prove three statements using different techniques: 'if n is even, then n^2 is even' (direct), 'if n^2 is odd, then n is odd' (contrapositive), 'the square root of 2 is irrational' (contradiction)

review.value: Proof by contradiction is most useful when the statement to prove is negative ('there is no X such that...') or when direct approaches do not reveal a clear path; assume the opposite exists and derive something impossible

Every proof has three parts: (1) state what you are proving, (2) state your assumptions and proof strategy, (3) proceed through logical steps to the conclusion. Each step must follow from previous steps or known results

learning_objectives: Perform set operations: union, intersection, difference, complement
Determine whether a function is injective, surjective, or bijective
Classify relations as reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and identify equivalence relations

lecture_notes.introduction: Sets, functions, and relations are the building blocks of mathematics and computer science. Database queries are set operations, type systems are functions, and equivalence classes partition data into meaningful groups.
key_concepts: Set notation, membership, subsets, power sets
Set operations: union, intersection, difference, symmetric difference
Functions: domain, codomain, range, injective, surjective, bijective
Relations: properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive, antisymmetric)
Equivalence relations and equivalence classes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a database query as set operations, show that modular arithmetic defines an equivalence relation, and prove that a hash function is a function (but not injective)

review.value: A bijection (one-to-one and onto) between two sets proves they have the same size; this is the foundation of counting arguments and is why we can prove that the reals are uncountable (no bijection with naturals exists)

An equivalence relation on a set divides it into non-overlapping equivalence classes that cover the entire set; this is used in type checking (types are equivalence classes of expressions), modular arithmetic, and database normalization

class_schedule.topic: Counting: Permutations, Combinations, and the Pigeonhole Principle
learning_objectives: Apply the product rule, sum rule, and inclusion-exclusion for counting
Calculate permutations and combinations for selection problems
Use the pigeonhole principle to prove existence results
readings: Lehman, Mathematics for Computer Science, Ch. 15-16

lecture_notes.introduction: Counting is essential for algorithm analysis (how many operations?), probability (what fraction of outcomes?), and cryptography (how many possible keys?). Combinatorics provides systematic tools for counting without enumerating every case.
key_concepts: Product rule: if task A has m ways and task B has n ways, together there are m*n ways
Sum rule: if A and B are disjoint, total ways = ways for A + ways for B
Permutations: ordered arrangements, P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!
Combinations: unordered selections, C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!)
Pigeonhole principle: if n items go into m boxes and n > m, at least one box has more than one item
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the number of valid passwords, the number of 5-card poker hands, and use the pigeonhole principle to prove that in any group of 13 people, at least 2 share a birth month

review.value: |A union B| = |A| + |B| - |A intersect B|; this corrects for double-counting when sets overlap. Extends to three or more sets with alternating signs

If a hash table has n keys and m < n buckets, at least one bucket has multiple keys (collision guaranteed). If a program has n possible states and runs for n+1 steps, it must revisit a state (loop detection)

learning_objectives: Prove properties of graphs using degree-sum formula and Euler's formula
Apply graph algorithms: coloring, matching, Euler and Hamilton paths

lecture_notes.introduction: Graphs are the most versatile data structure in computer science. Social networks, road maps, dependency chains, scheduling constraints, and circuit boards are all graphs. Graph theory provides the mathematical foundation for the graph algorithms you learned in data structures.
key_concepts: Graph terminology: vertex, edge, degree, path, cycle, connected
Degree-sum formula: sum of all degrees = 2 * number of edges
Euler path and circuit: existence conditions
Graph coloring: chromatic number and applications to scheduling
Planar graphs and Euler's formula: V - E + F = 2
Trees: connected acyclic graphs, n-1 edges for n vertices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model a class scheduling problem as a graph coloring problem: courses that share students cannot be at the same time. Find the chromatic number to determine the minimum number of time slots needed.

review.value: The sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges; this immediately proves that the number of vertices with odd degree must be even. This simple result is the basis for many graph theory proofs

Trees are connected graphs with no cycles; they have exactly n-1 edges for n vertices. Binary trees, spanning trees, parse trees, and decision trees are all applications. Adding any edge creates exactly one cycle; removing any edge disconnects the tree

questions.question: Prove by contradiction that there is no largest prime number.
questions.answer: Assume for contradiction that there is a largest prime p. Consider the number N = p! + 1 (the product of all integers from 1 to p, plus 1). N is not divisible by any integer from 2 to p, because dividing N by any such integer leaves a remainder of 1. Therefore N is either prime itself (and larger than p) or has a prime factor larger than p. In either case, there exists a prime larger than p, contradicting our assumption. Therefore there is no largest prime.

questions.question: Using truth tables or logical equivalences, prove that (P implies Q) is logically equivalent to (NOT P OR Q).
questions.answer:
Truth table method:
P | Q | P->Q | NOT P | NOT P OR Q
T | T |  T   |   F   |     T
T | F |  F   |   F   |     F
F | T |  T   |   T   |     T
F | F |  T   |   T   |     T

Since columns 3 and 5 are identical for all truth value combinations, P->Q is logically equivalent to NOT P OR Q.

questions.question: A computer password must be 8 characters long, using uppercase letters (26), lowercase letters (26), and digits (10). It must contain at least one digit. How many valid passwords are there?
questions.answer: Total characters available: 26 + 26 + 10 = 62. Total passwords of length 8 with no restrictions: 62^8. Passwords with NO digits (only letters): 52^8. Passwords with at least one digit: 62^8 - 52^8 = 218,340,105,584,896 - 53,459,728,531,456 = 164,880,377,053,440.

rubric.key: Correct calculation of total and letter-only passwords

questions.question: A connected graph has 8 vertices. The degree sequence is: 4, 3, 3, 3, 2, 2, 2, 1. Does this graph exist? Justify your answer using the handshaking lemma.
questions.answer: Sum of degrees = 4 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 20. By the handshaking lemma, sum of degrees = 2 * |E|, so |E| = 10. The sum is even, so the degree sequence passes the basic necessary condition. However, we must also verify realizability. The vertex of degree 4 must connect to 4 of the 7 other vertices. The vertex of degree 1 connects to exactly 1 vertex. This is realizable (can be verified by the Erdos-Gallai theorem or by construction). So yes, this graph can exist.

rubric.key: Correct degree sum and edge count
Considers realizability beyond just even sum

major_assignments.title: Discrete Mathematics in Computer Science Applications
major_assignments.description: Apply discrete mathematics concepts to three computer science problems: (1) design and prove correct a hash function using modular arithmetic, (2) analyze a network topology using graph theory, and (3) calculate probabilities for a security analysis using combinatorics.
objectives: Apply proof techniques to verify algorithm correctness
Use graph theory to analyze network properties
Apply combinatorics to calculate probabilities for security analysis
Connect mathematical concepts to practical CS applications
deliverables: Written solutions with formal proofs for all three problems
Python implementations demonstrating each mathematical concept
Analysis report (3-4 pages) connecting theory to practice
Visualizations of graph properties and probability distributions

criteria: All proofs are logically valid with no gaps
Proof technique is appropriate for each statement
Mathematical notation is used correctly
Assumptions are stated explicitly
Hash function analysis uses modular arithmetic correctly
Graph analysis identifies relevant properties (connectivity, diameter, etc.)
Probability calculations use correct combinatorial formulas
All numerical answers are correct
Python code correctly demonstrates each concept
Code is clean and well-documented
Visualizations effectively illustrate the mathematics
All code runs without errors
Report connects theory to practical CS applications
Writing is clear and mathematically precise
Sources are properly cited
Professional formatting
Rigorous proofs demonstrating mathematical maturity
Correct application of discrete math to CS problems
Clean implementations with effective visualizations
Clear documentation connecting theory to practice

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of mathematical concepts and proof skills

homework_assignments.description: Weekly problem sets with proofs and calculations

major_assignments.description: Application project connecting math to CS

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering logic, proofs, sets, and counting

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all discrete math topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late homework loses 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of problem-solving approaches is encouraged. All written proofs and solutions must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying proofs from solution manuals or other students is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a calculator and access to a proof assistant or LaTeX for typesetting proofs (recommended but not required).
